metadata.name: ukiyo-e seascape #80
metadata.description:
Generative seascape (or lakescape) inspired by ukiyo-e art.
The art is programmatically drawn using basic shapes, easy math, random and noise. (except text typography)

February 2022
ykxotkx

metadata.name: Geode #159
metadata.description:
A single seed shape is chosen from 13, and placed onto the plane. At every step, a copy of this seed is laid edge-to-edge adjacent to an existing shape, without causing any overlaps. This copy is colored based on the edge it shares with the existing shape. The process continues until the animation completes.

New shapes are placed closest to a moving target. In some mints, this target is slow moving, causing new shapes to expand radially from the seed. In other mints, this target races around, causing new shapes to chase it. 

Each of the 13 shapes forms different patterns under different circumstances. Not all patterns will be explored.

Controls:
  - SPACE: Pause/unpause the animation.
  - ENTER: Skip the animation.
  - R: Restart the animation.

URL Params:
  - "preview=1": Skip the animation.
  - "flex=1": Adapt the boundaries to the size of the window.
  - “slow=1”: Draw only one shape per frame.

Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0

Structures in construction.

Press 's' in full screen mode to save a 1280x2024 version.
Press 'h' in full screen mode to save a 2560x4096 version.

metadata.name: WTBS: Olympics Poster Edition #121
metadata.description:
WTBS: Olympics Poster Edition is our official collaboration with Jeres, who was kind enough to work with us in support of the show. The project leans into classic Olympic poster design motifs, embellished by Jeres' signature approach to color and shape. Huge thanks to Jeres for donating their time and enthusiasm to this one, we hope you enjoy it!

As with all WTBS projects, your minting will directly support the podcast and keep us going (and more importantly, collecting!). Our goal is to keep the show free from ads and fully supported by the community, your donations and contributions continue to make that possible. Thank you in advance to everyone who chooses to mint!

Jeres: I love vintage posters. When going to second-hand shops, flea markets or wherever people are selling their used goods, I’m always on the lookout for what people used to hang on their walls. Bauhaus-inspired posters, socialist propaganda, Nagel prints, anything art deco, etc.. but whenever I come across something Olympics inspired I always feel like I hit pay dirt. There’s something about the clean, simple design being used to show people coming together along with the undertones of human excellence that just feels special. And circles. I love circles. I hope you enjoy this collection as much as I enjoyed putting it together with Trinity and Will.

Approximate rarity notes:
5%: Gold Edition
10%: Silver Edition
15%: Bronze Edition 

Commands in fullscreen mode:
S : Save current resolution
1 : Save 2000x3000px
2 : Save 4000x6000px
3 : Save 6000x9000px
4 : Save 8000x12000px
5 : Save 10000x15000px

(The higher the resolution, the longer it takes to render/save. use Chrome for best results.)

metadata.name: Garden, Monoliths #191
metadata.description:
"Garden, Monoliths" is the result of an algorithm that started in April 2021. It was called "Grass.js".
Spring was blooming all around the house, taking over the stones and remnants of ancient battles that stood here. Inspiration was everywhere.
The initial goal was to use mathematical formulas for creating patterns inspired by the density of nature, that could be used by a pen plotter, so it kept focusing on outputting vector lines. This history and practice produced a graphical style based on line strokes, plus occasionally some transparent, filled shapes.
Each artwork will be either "Garden" or "Monolith", for they are variations of two existing designs from the workshop.

metadata.name: Orchard #203
metadata.description:
A natural media rendering of a fruit tree during one of the four seasons. Sometimes, you'll catch it at night - with lights strung on the tree!

The amount of color in spring and fall, snow in winter, fruit on and off the tree will all vary. There are four spring bloom palettes, four trunk colors, and seven types of fruit. 

Press ’s’ to save a screenshot. Image size is 1000x1000px
Press ’p’ to pause/resume.

To render a variation at a new size, open live view and append '?size=#' to the end of the URL. The new size may be from 100 to 3,000. This version will not be a 1:1 match to your edition but will have all of the same features.

Copyright (c) 2022 Matt Perkins, hello@mattperkins.me, @nudoru on Twitter. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, see LICENSE.txt for more information.
